teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Acceptance is an unqualified expression of assent to the terms of an offer. For a bilateral contract to be formed, acceptance must generally be communicated to the offeror.

**Silence Cannot Constitute Acceptance:** In *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869, an uncle wrote to his nephew offering to buy a horse, stating: 'If I hear no more about him, I consider the horse mine at £30 15s.' The nephew intended to accept but did not communicate this. The auctioneer (Bindley) later sold the horse by mistake. The uncle sued for conversion, but the court held there was no contract — the nephew had not communicated acceptance. The offeror cannot unilaterally impose a contract by deeming silence to be acceptance.

**Acceptance by Conduct:** In *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] 2 App Cas 666, Brogden had supplied coal for years without a formal contract. The railway sent a draft agreement; Brogden amended it (a counter-offer), signed, and returned it. The railway's agent filed it without formal acceptance. For two years thereafter, both parties ordered and supplied coal on the terms of the draft. The House of Lords held a contract existed — the railway's conduct in continuing to order and accept coal on the new terms constituted acceptance of Brogden's counter-offer.

**Unilateral Contracts:** In unilateral contracts, acceptance is by performance of the requested act, and communication of acceptance may be waived: *Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co* [1893] 1 QB 256.

**In short:** Acceptance must be communicated (or performed in unilateral contracts). Silence is never acceptance — the offeror cannot impose it. Conduct may constitute acceptance where it is unequivocal.
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869
Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256

question: __________ v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869 established that silence cannot constitute acceptance, even where the offeror states that silence will be treated as acceptance.
feedback: Correct. *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869 is the leading authority on silence and acceptance. The uncle's statement that silence = acceptance was ineffective — the nephew never communicated acceptance, so no contract existed.
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question: __________ v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666 established that acceptance may be inferred from conduct, even without express communication of acceptance.
feedback: Correct. *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] 2 App Cas 666 — the railway's act of continuing to order and accept coal on the terms of the draft agreement constituted acceptance by conduct. No formal communication of acceptance was necessary.
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666

question: Match each case to its principle on acceptance:
answer: Silence cannot constitute acceptance=An offeree must positively communicate acceptance — the offeror cannot deem silence to be acceptance; Acceptance by conduct is valid=Conduct that unequivocally indicates assent can constitute binding acceptance; Unilateral acceptance by performance=In unilateral contracts, performing the requested act constitutes acceptance without prior communication; Communication is essential for bilateral contracts=Generally, acceptance must be communicated to the offeror for a bilateral contract to be formed

options.text: An offeree must positively communicate acceptance — the offeror cannot deem silence to be acceptance
Conduct that unequivocally indicates assent can constitute binding acceptance
In unilateral contracts, performing the requested act constitutes acceptance without prior communication
Generally, acceptance must be communicated to the offeror for a bilateral contract to be formed
options.pair: Communication is essential for bilateral contracts

feedback: Well matched. The four foundational principles of acceptance: (1) communication is required in bilateral contracts, (2) silence is never enough (*Felthouse v Bindley* [1862]), (3) conduct may suffice (*Brogden* [1877]), and (4) unilateral contracts accept by performance (*Carlill* [1893]).
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869
Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256

question: Eliza emails her cousin James: 'I'll sell you my holiday cottage in Cornwall for £150,000. If I don't hear back by Friday 5pm, I'll take it you agree and instruct my solicitor.' James reads the email, decides to accept, but forgets to reply. On Saturday, Eliza instructs her solicitor to draft the transfer. James refuses to proceed, saying he changed his mind. Applying *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862]:

options.text: James is bound — Eliza's email clearly stated silence would be acceptance, and James read it
options.feedback: Incorrect. Under *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862], the offeror cannot unilaterally impose acceptance by silence. Reading the email without replying is not acceptance.

options.text: James is bound — his decision to accept counts as mental acceptance, which is sufficient
options.feedback: Incorrect. A purely mental decision to accept, without communication or conduct manifesting that decision, is not valid acceptance in a bilateral contract.

options.text: James is not bound — silence cannot constitute acceptance, and the offeror cannot deem an offeree's inaction to be consent
options.feedback: Correct. *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869 — an offeror cannot impose a contract by stating silence = acceptance. James was under no duty to reply; his silence, even combined with a mental intention to accept, did not form a contract.

options.text: James is bound — an email offer with a clear deadline creates an obligation to reply, and failure to do so is acceptance by conduct
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no general duty to reply to offers. The *Felthouse* principle remains: the offeror cannot convert silence into acceptance.

feedback: The core principle from *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869 is that a contractual obligation cannot be thrust upon a person by deeming their silence to be acceptance. James's silence cannot be converted into a contract, regardless of what Eliza's email said.
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'Under English contract law, a party who receives an offer will be bound if they read the offer and decide internally to accept, even if they never communicate their acceptance. Additionally, an offeror may validly state in the offer that the offeree's silence will be deemed acceptance, thereby creating a binding contract without the offeree taking any positive action.'
answer: A purely internal decision to accept does not constitute valid acceptance — acceptance must be communicated to the offeror (or performed in unilateral contracts). Furthermore, an offeror cannot unilaterally impose acceptance by silence: *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869. The offeree must positively accept.
feedback: You found both errors. First, acceptance requires external manifestation — a mental decision alone is insufficient. Second, the offeror cannot contractually bind the offeree through silence. *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869 is the direct authority: even where the offer stated silence = acceptance, no contract arose because the offeree had not communicated acceptance.
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question: Which TWO of the following correctly state the law on acceptance in English contract law?

options.text: In a bilateral contract, acceptance must generally be communicated to the offeror
options.feedback: Correct. Communication of acceptance is the default rule for bilateral contracts — the offeror must be made aware of the acceptance.

options.text: An offeror may validly provide that the offeree's silence or inaction will be treated as acceptance
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] — the offeror cannot impose a contract by deeming silence to be acceptance. The offeree must positively assent.

options.text: Acceptance may be inferred from the offeree's conduct where the conduct unequivocally demonstrates assent
options.feedback: Correct. *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] — continuing to order and accept goods on the terms of the offer constituted acceptance by conduct.

options.text: If an offeree reads an offer and decides internally to accept, a contract is formed even without communication
options.feedback: Incorrect. A purely mental decision does not constitute acceptance. The offeree must communicate their intention to accept or perform the requested act.

options.text: Silence can amount to acceptance if the offer concerns goods worth less than £500
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no monetary threshold for the silence rule. *Felthouse* [1862] applies regardless of the value of the subject matter.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). Communication is the general rule for bilateral contracts; conduct may replace express communication where unequivocal (*Brogden* [1877]). Silence is never enough (*Felthouse v Bindley* [1862]).
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869
Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666

question: Farah runs a bakery. A local café emails her: 'Please deliver 50 sourdough loaves each Monday for 12 weeks at £2 each.' Farah does not reply but starts delivering 50 loaves every Monday. The café accepts and pays for the first 8 deliveries. On week 9, Farah stops delivering, claiming there was never a formal contract because she never communicated acceptance. Applying *Brogden* [1877]:

options.text: Farah is correct — she never replied to the offer, so no contract was formed
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Brogden* [1877] shows express communication of acceptance is not always required — conduct may suffice.

options.text: A contract was formed by Farah's conduct — her act of delivering the loaves and the café's acceptance and payment constituted mutual assent by conduct
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] 2 App Cas 666, Farah's conduct in commencing and continuing deliveries, and the café's conduct in accepting and paying, demonstrated a contract by conduct. Her failure to email a reply does not prevent formation.

options.text: A contract was formed but only from the date Farah sent her first invoice
options.feedback: Incorrect. The conduct started with the first delivery, not the invoice. The continuing performance evidenced acceptance from the outset.

options.text: Farah's silence plus her deliveries does not amount to acceptance because silence is never enough, even with conduct
options.feedback: Incorrect. This conflates silence alone (Felthouse) with silence plus conduct (Brogden). Farah's deliveries were positive conduct, not mere silence.

feedback: The distinction is critical: *Felthouse* [1862] concerns pure silence; *Brogden* [1877] concerns conduct. Farah's silence was not the relevant act — her deliveries were. The continuing course of dealing demonstrated mutual assent. Farah's later refusal to deliver is likely a breach of contract.
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question: In *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862], the uncle's statement that 'If I hear no more about him, I consider the horse mine' was ineffective because _________ cannot constitute acceptance.
feedback: Correct. The central holding of *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869 is that silence, even when the offeror states it will constitute acceptance, does not form a contract. The offeree must communicate acceptance positively.
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** For instantaneous forms of communication — telex, telephone, fax, and email — acceptance takes effect when and where it is received by the offeror, not when it is sent. This is the 'receipt rule' and it is the opposite of the postal rule.

**Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327:** Entores in London made a telex offer to Miles Far East Corp in Amsterdam. The Dutch company accepted by telex. Lord Denning held that the contract was formed in London, where the acceptance was received. A telex is instantaneous — the parties are in the same position as if they were in the same room. The postal rule, which provides that acceptance is effective on posting (Adams v Lindsell (1818)), does not apply to instantaneous communication.

**Email:** Under English law, email acceptance follows the receipt rule — it is effective when it reaches the offeror's server and is available to be read. The fact the offeror does not read it immediately does not prevent the contract from forming at that moment.

**Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, reg 11:** For electronic orders via a website, the order and any acknowledgment of receipt are deemed received when the parties to whom they are addressed are able to access them.

**Click-wrap & Browse-wrap:** Click-wrap — where a user clicks 'I agree' to terms — constitutes valid acceptance. Browse-wrap — where terms are accessible via hyperlink without requiring active consent — is more precarious; acceptance may not be inferred from mere use unless the user had reasonable notice of the terms.

**Fax:** Treated as instantaneous — acceptance by fax is effective when received at the offeror's fax machine.

**Jurisdiction:** The place where acceptance is received determines the place of contract formation, which in turn determines jurisdiction. This is significant for international commercial contracts.

**In short:** Instantaneous communications: receipt rule. Postal communications: postal rule. The distinction matters for timing, jurisdiction, and the offeree's ability to withdraw.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, reg 11
Adams v Lindsell (1818) 1 B & Ald 681

question: __________ Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327 established the receipt rule for instantaneous communications: acceptance by telex takes effect when and where it is received, not when it is sent.
feedback: Correct. *Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp* [1955] 2 QB 327 — Lord Denning held that telex communication is instantaneous, so acceptance is effective when received in London. The postal rule (*Adams v Lindsell*) did not apply.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327

question: Under regulation __________ of the Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, where a service provider is obliged to acknowledge receipt of an electronic order, the order and the acknowledgment are deemed received when the parties are able to access them.
feedback: Correct. Regulation 11 of the Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2013) governs the deemed time of receipt for electronic orders and acknowledgments. Both are effective when accessible to the intended recipient.
oscoaReferences: Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, SI 2002/2013, reg 11

question: Match each method of communication to the rule governing when acceptance takes effect:
answer: Acceptance when received by offeror=Telex, email, and fax are instantaneous; acceptance is effective when and where received; Acceptance when posted=Postal letters follow the postal rule; acceptance is effective when the letter is posted; Acceptance when user clicks 'I agree'=Click-wrap contracts — acceptance occurs at the moment of the click; Acceptance by performance without prior notice=In unilateral contracts, performance of the requested act is acceptance

options.text: Telex, email, and fax are instantaneous; acceptance is effective when and where received
Postal letters follow the postal rule; acceptance is effective when the letter is posted
Click-wrap contracts — acceptance occurs at the moment of the click
In unilateral contracts, performance of the requested act is acceptance
options.pair: Acceptance by performance without prior notice

feedback: Well matched. The critical distinction is between instantaneous and non-instantaneous communication. The postal rule (*Adams v Lindsell* (1818)) is an exception that applies only to postal acceptance; all other forms of acceptance communication follow the receipt rule (*Entores* [1955]).
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Adams v Lindsell (1818) 1 B & Ald 681

question: A London-based exporter emails an offer to sell 500 tonnes of grain to a New York buyer at 9am GMT. The buyer in New York emails acceptance at 9:15am GMT. Due to a transatlantic internet outage, the acceptance email is delayed and does not reach the London exporter's server until 10:45am GMT. At 10:30am GMT, having received no response, the exporter sells the grain to a third party and emails New York: 'Offer withdrawn.' Applying the receipt rule from *Entores* [1955]:

options.text: A contract was formed at 9:15am GMT when the buyer sent the acceptance — the postal rule applies to email
options.feedback: Incorrect. Email is not governed by the postal rule. The receipt rule applies to instantaneous and electronic communications — *Entores* [1955].

options.text: A contract was formed at 10:45am and the exporter's prior sale is a breach of that contract
options.feedback: Close, but the withdrawal was communicated at 10:30am, before the acceptance was received at 10:45am. The withdrawal was effective first.

options.text: No contract — an email acceptance is never effective unless the sender receives a read receipt
options.feedback: Incorrect. Read receipts are not required for an acceptance by email to be effective. The acceptance is effective when it reaches the offeror's server.

options.text: No contract — the acceptance was not received until 10:45am, but the withdrawal was effectively communicated at 10:30am, before the acceptance took effect
options.feedback: Correct. Under the receipt rule, acceptance takes effect when received (10:45am). The withdrawal was received earlier (10:30am), so it was effective before the acceptance. No contract formed.

feedback: Under the receipt rule from *Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp* [1955] 2 QB 327, email acceptance is effective only when received. The exporter's withdrawal arrived at 10:30am and was effective immediately. The buyer's acceptance only became effective at 10:45am — but the offer had already been withdrawn. Note: this illustrates the risk asymmetry for the offeree in instantaneous communications — unlike the postal rule, the offeree bears the risk of delay.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'Under English contract law, the postal rule applies to all forms of distance communication, including telex, email, and fax. This means acceptance by telex is effective when the sender dispatches the telex, not when the offeror receives it. The Electronic Commerce Regulations have codified the postal rule for all electronic transactions.'
answer: The postal rule does NOT apply to telex, email, fax, or other instantaneous forms of communication. Acceptance by telex, email and fax is effective when received, not when sent (*Entores* [1955] 2 QB 327). The Electronic Commerce Regulations 2002 apply a receipt rule, not the postal rule.
feedback: Found it. The error confuses the postal rule (applicable only to postal acceptance: *Adams v Lindsell* (1818)) with the receipt rule (applicable to instantaneous and electronic communications: *Entores* [1955]). The Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, reg 11, provides that orders and acknowledgments are deemed received when accessible — a receipt-based standard, not a postal rule.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Adams v Lindsell (1818) 1 B & Ald 681
Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, reg 11

question: Which TWO of the following correctly state the law on acceptance by instantaneous or electronic communication under English law?

options.text: A telex acceptance takes effect when it is received and printed out by the offeror's telex machine
options.feedback: Correct. *Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp* [1955] 2 QB 327 — telex acceptance is effective when received, and the contract is formed at the place of receipt.

options.text: The postal rule governs all forms of electronic acceptance, including email and fax
options.feedback: Incorrect. The postal rule is confined to postal communications. Instantaneous and electronic communications follow the receipt rule.

options.text: An offeror cannot withdraw an offer sent by email under any circumstances, because electronic offers are irrevocable
options.feedback: Incorrect. An offer may be withdrawn at any time before acceptance, regardless of the medium. The withdrawal must be communicated to the offeree before acceptance takes effect.

options.text: The place where acceptance is received determines the jurisdiction in which the contract is formed
options.feedback: Correct. *Entores* [1955] — the contract was formed in London where the acceptance was received, giving English courts jurisdiction. The receipt rule determines both timing and place of formation.

options.text: Under the Electronic Commerce Regulations 2002, an email acceptance is effective only when the offeror opens and reads it
options.feedback: Incorrect. The regulations deem receipt when the communication is accessible, not when it is actually read. The same principle applies at common law.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). *Entores* [1955] establishes both the timing and the jurisdictional consequence of the receipt rule. The receipt rule, not the postal rule, governs instantaneous communication. The place of receipt is the place of contract formation.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002, reg 11

question: SoftSys Ltd sells software online. A customer clicks a button labelled 'Buy Now — £49.99' on the product page (Step 1). The customer is then directed to a page displaying the full terms and conditions, with a checkbox stating 'I have read and agree to the Terms and Conditions' and a button 'Complete Purchase' (Step 2). The customer checks the box and clicks the button. The terms contain a clause limiting SoftSys's liability to £5. Applying the principles of click-wrap acceptance:

options.text: No contract was formed — the customer only clicked buttons, which is insufficient for legal acceptance
options.feedback: Incorrect. Clicking 'I agree' after being presented with terms is the electronic equivalent of signing a contract — this is binding click-wrap acceptance.

options.text: A contract was formed by click-wrap acceptance when the customer clicked 'Complete Purchase' after checking the agreement box
options.feedback: Correct. This is a classic click-wrap contract. The customer was required to take a positive step (checking the box and clicking the button) indicating assent to the terms. This constitutes valid acceptance — the equivalent of signature.

options.text: No contract was formed because the customer did not actually read the terms and conditions
options.feedback: Incorrect. Under the signature rule (*L'Estrange v Graucob* [1934]), and by analogy in click-wrap, a party is bound by terms they sign or click to accept, even without reading them.

options.text: A contract was formed on the product page (Step 1) when the customer clicked 'Buy Now', and the later terms are not incorporated
options.feedback: Incorrect. The 'Buy Now' button was only an invitation to proceed. The contract was formed at Step 2 when the customer positively accepted the terms. The two-step process is effective.

feedback: Click-wrap contracts — where the user must take a positive action to indicate assent to terms presented on screen — are generally valid and binding in English law. The checkbox and 'Complete Purchase' button together constitute acceptance. The fact the customer did not read the terms is no defence under the *L'Estrange v Graucob* [1934] principle, subject to statutory protections (e.g., Consumer Rights Act 2015).
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
L'Estrange v Graucob [1934] 2 KB 394

question: Arrange the sequence of events in *Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp* [1955] in the correct order as they relate to contract formation:
answer: Entores (London) sends telex offer to Miles Far East Corp (Amsterdam) → Miles Far East Corp receives the telex offer in Amsterdam → Miles Far East Corp sends telex acceptance from Amsterdam to London → Entores receives the telex acceptance in London — CONTRACT FORMED

options.text: Entores (London) sends telex offer to Miles Far East Corp (Amsterdam)
Miles Far East Corp receives the telex offer in Amsterdam
Miles Far East Corp sends telex acceptance from Amsterdam to London
Entores receives the telex acceptance in London — CONTRACT FORMED

feedback: Correct. The critical point is step 4: the contract was formed in London when the acceptance was received. Lord Denning analogised telex to two parties in the same room — acceptance is effective when heard, which for telex means when received. The postal rule was held inapplicable.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Acceptance may be communicated by conduct, through a prescribed method, or (in unilateral contracts) by performance. Where the offeror does not prescribe a method, any method that reasonably communicates acceptance suffices. Where the offeror does prescribe a method, the question is whether the method is mandatory or merely permissive.

**Acceptance by Conduct:** *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] 2 App Cas 666 remains the leading authority. The railway's conduct — continuing to order and accept coal on the terms of the draft — constituted acceptance even though the railway never formally communicated it. Conduct that unequivocally indicates assent is sufficient.

**Prescribed Methods of Acceptance:** In *Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd* [1970] 1 WLR 241, the offeror stated that acceptance should be sent to the surveyor by a certain date. The offeree sent acceptance to the offeror's solicitors instead. The court held the prescription was not mandatory — the method was intended to facilitate, not to exclude other methods. Principle: a prescribed method is only mandatory if the offeror makes it clear (expressly or by necessary implication) that no other method will suffice. An acceptance by an equally efficacious or more expeditious method may be valid.

**Waiver of Communication:** In unilateral contracts, the offeror may waive the requirement for communication of acceptance. *Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co* [1893] 1 QB 256 — Mrs Carlill was not required to notify the company before using the smoke ball. Performance was acceptance.

**Must Be Communicated to the Offeror:** As a general rule, acceptance in a bilateral contract must be communicated to the offeror personally (or their authorised agent). Communication to a third party or to someone without authority to receive it is not effective.

**In short:** Conduct suffices where unequivocal (*Brogden*). A prescribed method is not mandatory unless clearly exclusive (*Manchester Diocesan Council*). Unilateral contracts waive the communication requirement (*Carlill*).
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241
Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256

question: __________ Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241 held that a prescribed method of acceptance is only mandatory if the offeror makes it clear that no other method will suffice.
feedback: Correct. *Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd* [1970] 1 WLR 241 — the direction to send acceptance to the surveyor was permissive, not mandatory. The acceptance sent to the solicitors was valid.
oscoaReferences: Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241

question: Match each case to the principle it establishes on methods of acceptance:
answer: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway=A contract may be formed by continuing conduct without formal communication; Manchester Diocesan Council v C&G Investments=A prescribed method is not mandatory unless the offeror states it is the exclusive method; Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball=The requirement to communicate acceptance may be waived in unilateral contracts; Felthouse v Bindley=The offeror cannot impose a contract by deeming silence to be acceptance

options.text: A contract may be formed by continuing conduct without formal communication
A prescribed method is not mandatory unless the offeror states it is the exclusive method
options.pair: Manchester Diocesan Council v C&G Investments

options.text: The requirement to communicate acceptance may be waived in unilateral contracts
The offeror cannot impose a contract by deeming silence to be acceptance

feedback: Well matched. These four cases capture the spectrum of acceptance methods: conduct (*Brogden*), prescribed methods (*Manchester*), waiver of communication in unilateral contracts (*Carlill*), and the floor — silence is never enough (*Felthouse*).
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241
Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question: GreenTech Ltd offers to purchase Liam's software business for £2 million. The offer letter states: 'To accept, please deliver a signed copy of this letter to our solicitors, Higgs & Co, by 5pm on 30 March.' Liam signs the letter, but instead of delivering it to Higgs & Co, he emails a scanned copy directly to GreenTech's CEO at 3pm on 30 March. The CEO reads it immediately but later refuses to proceed, saying Liam used the wrong method. Applying *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970]:

options.text: Liam's acceptance is invalid — the prescribed method must be strictly followed, and email was not delivery to Higgs & Co
options.feedback: Incorrect. Under *Manchester* [1970], a prescribed method is not automatically mandatory. The question is whether the language indicates exclusivity.

options.text: Liam's acceptance is likely valid — the prescribed method was not expressed as exclusive, and email to the CEO was an equally (if not more) efficacious form of communication
options.feedback: Correct. Applying *Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd* [1970] 1 WLR 241, the direction to deliver to Higgs & Co was permissive rather than mandatory. The words 'To accept, please deliver' suggest a preference, not an exclusive requirement. Liam's email was equally efficacious — it conveyed acceptance immediately to the decision-maker.

options.text: Liam's acceptance is invalid because all prescribed methods are mandatory under English law
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Manchester* [1970] is clear that not all prescribed methods are mandatory — it depends on the construction of the offer.

options.text: Liam's acceptance is valid only if Higgs & Co confirm receipt
options.feedback: Incorrect. The validity does not depend on Higgs & Co's confirmation. The acceptance was communicated to the offeror (the CEO is the decision-maker) and was effective on receipt.

feedback: The key distinction is between permissive language ('please deliver' — indicating a preferred method) and exclusive language ('must', 'only', 'exclusively' — indicating a mandatory method). *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970] held that the court should construe the offer to determine whether the method was intended to be the sole method of acceptance.
oscoaReferences: Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'If an offeror prescribes a particular method of acceptance — for example, acceptance must be sent by recorded delivery — the offeree must always follow that method exactly. Any deviation from the prescribed method renders the acceptance void regardless of whether the deviation was more efficient or whether the offeror received the acceptance. There is no discretion for the court to find otherwise.'
answer: This is overstated. A prescribed method is only mandatory if the offeror states or clearly implies that it is exclusive. An equally efficacious or more expeditious method may be valid: *Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd* [1970] 1 WLR 241. The court will construe the terms of the offer to determine whether exclusivity was intended.
feedback: Found it. The paragraph treats all prescribed methods as automatically mandatory. The correct position, from *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970], is that the court construes the terms of the offer to determine whether the method was intended to be exclusive. Language such as 'please' or 'I would prefer' indicates a non-mandatory prescription. Only express or necessarily implied exclusivity makes the method mandatory.
oscoaReferences: Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241

question: Which TWO of the following correctly state the law on acceptance by conduct and prescribed methods under English contract law?

options.text: If an offeree begins to perform the contract without formally communicating acceptance, that performance may constitute acceptance by conduct
options.feedback: Correct. *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] — the railway's continuing to order coal on the new terms was acceptance by conduct, despite no formal communication.

options.text: Any prescription of a method of acceptance in the offer is always mandatory and must be followed exactly for a valid acceptance
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970] — a prescribed method is only mandatory if the offer makes clear it is exclusive. A permissive method does not invalidate alternative equally efficacious acceptance.

options.text: In a unilateral contract, the offeror may waive the requirement to communicate acceptance — performance of the requested act itself constitutes acceptance
options.feedback: Correct. *Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co* [1893] — Mrs Carlill was not required to notify the company before using the smoke ball. Performance was acceptance, and communication was waived by the nature of the offer.

options.text: Acceptance must always be communicated to the offeror personally and can never be communicated through an agent
options.feedback: Incorrect. Acceptance may be communicated to an agent authorised to receive it. There is no rule against agency in acceptance — in fact, the *Manchester* [1970] case concerned acceptance sent to solicitors.

options.text: Conduct can only constitute acceptance if the offeree also writes a letter confirming their intention to be bound
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Brogden* [1877] itself involved no confirming letter — the conduct alone was sufficient. Written confirmation is not a legal prerequisite for acceptance by conduct.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). *Brogden* [1877] establishes acceptance by conduct; *Carlill* [1893] establishes waiver of communication in unilateral contracts. The other propositions are incorrect: prescribed methods are not automatically mandatory, and no confirming letter is required for acceptance by conduct.
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241

question: A pharmaceutical company places a newspaper advert: 'We will pay £50,000 to any qualified doctor who completes Stage 3 clinical trial training on our drug and reports the results to us by 31 December. No need to contact us in advance — just complete the training and report.' Dr Khan completes the training and reports the results on 28 December. The company refuses to pay, arguing Dr Khan never communicated acceptance before starting the training. Applying *Carlill* [1893]:

options.text: Dr Khan is entitled to the £50,000 — this is a unilateral contract; the company waived the requirement for communication, and Dr Khan accepted by performance
options.feedback: Correct. The advert mirrors *Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co* [1893] 1 QB 256 — a clear promise, no requirement for prior communication ('No need to contact us'), and acceptance by performing the requested act. Dr Khan's completion of the training and reporting was acceptance.

options.text: Dr Khan is not entitled because he should have emailed the company before starting the training
options.feedback: Incorrect. The advert expressly stated no prior contact was needed. This is a clear waiver of the communication requirement, exactly as in *Carlill* [1893].

options.text: Dr Khan is entitled to reasonable remuneration for his time but not the full £50,000
options.feedback: Incorrect. The offer was for £50,000, not for reasonable remuneration. If Dr Khan accepted by performance, he is entitled to the amount stated in the offer.

options.text: The advert was a mere invitation to treat, so no contract was formed
options.feedback: Incorrect. While advertisements are generally invitations to treat, *Carlill* [1893] carved out the exception for unilateral offers. The clear promise, the specificity of the task, and the waiver of communication indicate a binding unilateral offer.

feedback: The express statement 'No need to contact us in advance' is a waiver of the communication requirement. Under *Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co* [1893] 1 QB 256, the offeror may waive communication in a unilateral contract. Dr Khan accepted by performing the stipulated acts. He is entitled to the £50,000.
oscoaReferences: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256

question: Arrange the sequence of events in *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] in the correct order:
answer: Brogden supplies coal to Metropolitan Railway without a formal written contract → The railway sends a draft contract to Brogden → Brogden amends the draft (adds arbitrator's name), signs, and returns it — a counter-offer → The railway's agent files the draft in a drawer — no formal acceptance communicated → Both parties order and supply coal on the terms of the draft for two years (ACCEPTANCE BY CONDUCT) → The House of Lords holds a contract was formed by conduct

options.text: Brogden supplies coal to Metropolitan Railway without a formal written contract
The railway sends a draft contract to Brogden
Brogden amends the draft (adds arbitrator's name), signs, and returns it — a counter-offer
The railway's agent files the draft in a drawer — no formal acceptance communicated
Both parties order and supply coal on the terms of the draft for two years — ACCEPTANCE BY CONDUCT
The House of Lords holds a contract was formed by conduct

feedback: Correct. The critical stage is step 5: the railway's act of ordering coal and Brogden's act of supplying it, both on the terms of the draft, constituted mutual acceptance by conduct. The House of Lords in *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] 2 App Cas 666 held that a contract existed despite the absence of any formal communication of acceptance.
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666

question: In *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877], the House of Lords held that the railway's continuing to order and accept coal on the terms of the draft agreement was acceptance by __________.
feedback: Correct. The House of Lords found that by continuing to deal with Brogden on the terms of the amended draft, the railway had accepted Brogden's counter-offer by conduct. No formal communication was required. *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] 2 App Cas 666.
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] __________ v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869 is the leading authority that silence, even when the offeror states it shall constitute acceptance, cannot form a contract.
feedback: Correct. *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] 11 CBNS 869 — the uncle's letter stating 'If I hear no more' was ineffective. This consolidates the foundational Block A principle that silence is not acceptance.
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & B] Raj emails Imran at 2pm offering to sell his car for £8,000. Imran replies by email at 2:05pm: 'I accept.' Due to a network fault, the acceptance is delayed and is not received by Raj's server until 2:45pm. At 2:10pm, Raj emails Imran: 'I withdraw my offer — decided to keep the car.' This email is received immediately. Imran sues. Applying *Felthouse* [1862] and *Entores* [1955]:

options.text: A contract was formed at 2:05pm when Imran sent the acceptance — the postal rule applies to all emails
options.feedback: Incorrect. The postal rule does not govern email. *Entores* [1955] — electronic communications follow the receipt rule.

options.text: A contract was formed at 2:45pm — the acceptance was eventually received, and Raj's withdrawal was ineffective because Imran had already sent acceptance
options.feedback: Incorrect. The acceptance was only effective when received (2:45pm), but the revocation was received earlier (2:10pm). The offer was withdrawn before acceptance took effect.

options.text: No contract — under the receipt rule, Imran's acceptance was not effective until received (2:45pm), but Raj's withdrawal was communicated and effective at 2:10pm, extinguishing the offer before acceptance
options.feedback: Correct. This combines two principles: (1) under *Entores* [1955], email acceptance is effective on receipt, not on sending; (2) an offer may be withdrawn at any time before acceptance. Raj's withdrawal arrived before Imran's acceptance took effect. No contract. Imran bears the risk of delay in instantaneous communication.

options.text: Raj's withdrawal was invalid because he changed his mind without a legal reason
options.feedback: Incorrect. An offeror does not need a reason to withdraw an offer — an offer may be revoked at any time before acceptance (*Dickinson v Dodds* (1876)).

feedback: This consolidation item combines the receipt rule (*Entores* [1955]) with the rule on revocation of offers. Because email is treated as instantaneous, the acceptance was effective on receipt (2:45pm). The revocation was received before that (2:10pm). The offer was withdrawn before acceptance. Compare: if this were by post, the postal rule would make acceptance effective at 2:05pm (posting) and Raj would be in breach.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Dickinson v Dodds (1876) 2 Ch D 463

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A, B & C] Which TWO of the following propositions correctly state the law on methods of acceptance?

options.text: If an offeror prescribes a method of acceptance, any departure from that method automatically renders the acceptance void
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970] — the prescribed method is only mandatory if the offer makes clear it is exclusive. An equally efficacious alternative may be valid.

options.text: In a unilateral contract, acceptance is effected by performing the requested act, and the offeror may waive the requirement for prior communication of acceptance
options.feedback: Correct. *Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co* [1893] — Mrs Carlill accepted by using the smoke ball; no prior notice was required. This applies to Blocks A and C.

options.text: Acceptance by email is governed by the postal rule and is effective from the moment the sender clicks 'send'
options.feedback: Incorrect. Email acceptance follows the receipt rule (*Entores* [1955]). It is effective when received, not when sent. This is a Block B principle.

options.text: Conduct that unequivocally indicates assent can constitute acceptance even where the offeree does not formally communicate acceptance
options.feedback: Correct. *Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co* [1877] — the railway's conduct in continuing to deal on the new terms constituted acceptance. This is a Block A and C principle.

options.text: The offeror may validly stipulate that the offeree's silence after receiving the offer will be treated as acceptance
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Felthouse v Bindley* [1862] — the offeror cannot impose acceptance by silence. This is a foundational Block A principle.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). These span the key principles from all three blocks: unilateral contractual acceptance by performance and waiver of communication (*Carlill*); and acceptance by conduct without formal communication (*Brogden*). The other propositions misstate the law on prescribed methods, electronic acceptance, and silence.
oscoaReferences: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869
Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327

question:
[CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A, B & C] Spot the legal errors in this paragraph:

'Acceptance by email is governed by the postal rule, so a contract is formed the moment the offeree clicks send. Equally, acceptance by fax is governed by the postal rule and takes effect when the fax is deposited in the machine. The postal rule also applies to click-wrap contracts — a website user is deemed to accept the terms when they load the checkout page. And silence may be treated as acceptance provided the offeror stated this in the offer, because the offeree chose not to reply.'
answer: Four errors: (1) Email follows the receipt rule, not the postal rule — acceptance is effective when received (*Entores* [1955]). (2) Fax is instantaneous — acceptance effective when received, not when deposited. (3) Click-wrap is accepted when the user clicks 'I agree,' not when they load the page. (4) The offeror cannot impose acceptance by silence (*Felthouse v Bindley* [1862]) regardless of what the offer says.
feedback: Found them all. The paragraph conflates the postal rule (which applies only to postal communications) with the receipt rule (which applies to instantaneous and electronic communications). Email, fax, and click-wrap all follow the receipt rule — acceptance is effective on receipt, not on sending or on some other event. And the *Felthouse* principle on silence is absolute — the offeror cannot contractually bind the offeree through inaction.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & C] A supplier emails a manufacturer: 'We can supply 1,000 units at £12 each. Please confirm by return email if you wish to proceed.' The manufacturer does not reply by email but begins incorporating the supplier's units into its products and invoices the supplier for the first batch at £12 per unit. The supplier later claims no contract was formed because the manufacturer never emailed back. Applying *Brogden* [1877] and *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970]:

options.text: No contract — the supplier prescribed email as the method of acceptance, and the manufacturer's failure to email means acceptance was not communicated
options.feedback: Incorrect. 'Please confirm by return email' is permissive, not exclusive language. Under *Manchester* [1970], a prescribed method must be clearly mandatory to exclude other methods.

options.text: No contract — the manufacturer's silence (no email) plus its conduct (using the units) amounts to silence which cannot be acceptance under *Felthouse*
options.feedback: Incorrect. This conflates pure silence (Felthouse) with silence plus conduct (Brogden). The manufacturer's incorporation of the units was positive conduct, not mere silence.

options.text: A contract was formed because the supplier should have phoned to follow up
options.feedback: Incorrect. The question is whether the manufacturer's conduct constituted acceptance, not whether the supplier was diligent in follow-up.

options.text: A contract was formed — 'Please confirm by return email' was not a mandatory method, and the manufacturer's conduct in using and invoicing for the goods on the stated terms was acceptance by conduct
options.feedback: Correct. This draws on Block A (*Brogden* [1877] — acceptance by conduct) and Block C (*Manchester* [1970] — permissive language does not exclude other methods). The manufacturer's use of the units and invoicing at the exact unit price constituted unequivocal acceptance by conduct.

feedback: This integrates two principles: (1) *Brogden* [1877] — acceptance may be inferred from conduct that unequivocally demonstrates assent; and (2) *Manchester Diocesan Council* [1970] — permissive language ('please confirm') does not render the prescribed method exclusive. The manufacturer's conduct (using and invoicing at the same rate) was sufficient acceptance.
oscoaReferences: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] In *Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp* [1955] 2 QB 327, the contract was held to be formed in __________ because the telex acceptance was received there, giving the English courts jurisdiction.
feedback: Correct. *Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp* [1955] 2 QB 327 — the acceptance telex from Amsterdam was received in London, so the contract was formed in London. The receipt rule determines both timing and jurisdiction. This consolidates a key Block B principle.
oscoaReferences: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Match each case to its key holding on acceptance:
answer: Silence is never acceptance, even if the offer says it will be=The offeror cannot unilaterally impose contractual obligation through the offeree's inaction; Continuing to perform on the terms of a draft contract is acceptance by conduct=A contract may be formed without formal communication where the parties conduct themselves on the agreed terms; Telex acceptance takes effect when and where received — the receipt rule=Instantaneous communications are governed by the receipt rule, not the postal rule; A prescribed method of acceptance is not mandatory unless clearly exclusive=The court will construe the offer to determine whether the method was intended to be the sole permissible method; An advertisement offering a reward constitutes a unilateral offer; acceptance by performance=Unilateral contracts are accepted by performance, and the offeror may waive the requirement for prior communication

options.text: The offeror cannot unilaterally impose contractual obligation through the offeree's inaction
options.pair: Silence is never acceptance, even if the offer says it will be

options.text: A contract may be formed without formal communication where the parties conduct themselves on the agreed terms
options.pair: Continuing to perform on the terms of a draft contract is acceptance by conduct

options.text: Instantaneous communications are governed by the receipt rule, not the postal rule
options.pair: Telex acceptance takes effect when and where received — the receipt rule

options.text: The court will construe the offer to determine whether the method was intended to be the sole permissible method
options.pair: A prescribed method of acceptance is not mandatory unless clearly exclusive

options.text: Unilateral contracts are accepted by performance, and the offeror may waive the requirement for prior communication
options.pair: An advertisement offering a reward constitutes a unilateral offer; acceptance by performance

feedback: All matched correctly. This consolidation exercise covers the five key acceptance principles across all blocks: the silence rule (*Felthouse* [1862]), conduct acceptance (*Brogden* [1877]), the receipt rule (*Entores* [1955]), prescribed methods (*Manchester* [1970]), and unilateral acceptance (*Carlill* [1893]).
oscoaReferences: Felthouse v Bindley [1862] 11 CBNS 869
Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co [1877] 2 App Cas 666
Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corp [1955] 2 QB 327
Manchester Diocesan Council for Education v Commercial & General Investments Ltd [1970] 1 WLR 241
Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
